title: Brief Answers to the Big Questions

chapters.title: Forces that govern the universe sit beyond religious and cultural beliefs
chapters.text:
Why are we all here? Where did we all come from? Both religion and science offer answers to these fundamental questions, and they reach wildly different conclusions. Science argues that human existence is accidental, while religion suggests there is a built in meaning in human life.

These questions come from our deep wish to understand and explain the universe. In the earliest days, people leaned on religion to provide the answers. Before science could offer a logical and testable explanation for lightning, rain, and other natural phenomena, it was widely believed that gods were behind them.

The best way to understand science is not just to believe in it but to learn about it through research and evidence.

The game of tennis shows that the ball always lands where the natural laws of gravity and motion predict it will. There are no exceptions. Other factors do shape the result, such as the force of the swing or the speed of the wind, but those are simple data points, processed by the natural laws in a steady way to predict the outcome. These laws are universal.

The rotation of our planet obeys natural laws, and those laws cannot be broken by anyone. That means any God would have to obey them too. Theology diverges at this point with its claim of divine omnipotence. To bridge the gap between religious and scientific definitions of God, we have to see God and these universal laws as one and the same. That would mean defining God as the laws of nature themselves, rather than as a conscious being responsible for creating them.

chapters.title: Science and religion reach entirely different conclusions on the big questions
chapters.text:
The Big Bang theory is the leading cosmological explanation. It says the universe began as a very hot and dense singularity that expanded over 13.8 billion years to become the universe we know today. Most people do not find that description comforting, because they want to picture God as the creator of the universe. But when you look at the scale of the universe and compare it to the small and likely unintended nature of human life, the odds of a divine creator become tiny. If the traditional view of how the universe began is shaky, how did the universe actually begin?

People often cling to religion for comfort because they do not understand science or do not trust it enough.

The Big Bang theory says the universe was created from a dense point that expanded rapidly in nanoseconds. This story is the most popular creation narrative in the world today, and it all started from something smaller than a proton.

Edwin Hubble was the scientist who discovered that our universe is expanding, and he helped develop the Big Bang theory. He studied light from distant galaxies to measure whether they were moving and where they were going. His findings showed that the separation between the galaxies is growing, and the galaxies are increasing their speeds as they move further away from us.

The increasing speeds suggest they were once close together, perhaps some 15 billion years ago. It is possible that everything was a single point at the start.

So what was the world like before the Big Bang? Albert Einstein had a position on the issue that shed some light on this. He found that space and time are not fully separate, but are connected in a way we call space time. This fabric is the basis for the existence of the universe. Regions of high gravity, like the inside of a black hole, can warp the space time fabric. That warping can be so violent that time stops altogether.

chapters.title: A revolutionary theory about space helps answer what came before the Big Bang
chapters.text:
Going back to the very start, the cosmos began to contract, reaching an infinitely small and dense singularity. In that state, both space and time stop behaving as we classically understand them. This helps us answer what came before the Big Bang. Because space and time are warped at that point, there could not have been a cause for the Big Bang, since there was no time in which a cause could occur.

If Earth exists, we cannot rule out the possibility of other life forms outside this small blue world.

The earliest fossil evidence that proves life on this planet dates back 3.5 billion years. The Earth had been habitable 500 million years before that. When the Earth formed, the universe was already about 7 billion years old. That leaves room for an entire civilization to have risen and fallen long before our planet ever appeared.

There is enough time in the universe for alien life to have existed and vanished. That leads to the next question. Are there other planets that can sustain life?

For example, life on Earth took about 2.5 billion years to become intelligent. That is an extremely long stretch. What if other life forms simply never became intelligent before they went extinct because of some unlucky event? Maybe their star exploded, just as we expect our sun eventually will.

Star explosions are not the only threat alien life faces. We lost the dinosaurs to a small asteroid that crashed into Earth about 66 million years ago. Roughly speaking, those collisions happen once every 20 million years. That means we have been extremely lucky on this planet. Others may not have been so lucky, which could explain why we do not see signs of alien life.

The discovery of faint background microwaves in space in 1965 was the earliest evidence in support of the expanding universe theory.

chapters.title: Predictions are careful assumptions drawn from past and present experience
chapters.text:
Is it possible to predict the future? What would you do if you knew how you were going to die? These are fascinating ideas if they were possible. In the general sense, no one knows with certainty when they will die or what the winning lottery numbers will be. But there is a way in which this is theoretically possible.

Pierre Simon Laplace, a French scientist, argued that predicting the future was possible. He said that if you knew the positions and speeds of all the particles in the universe at a given moment, you could estimate where they would be at a future time.

That argument took a heavy hit in the twentieth century when Werner Heisenberg, a German physicist, presented what became known as the Uncertainty Principle. Heisenberg found that light comes in packets called quanta. Because of that, it is impossible to measure both the position and the speed of a particle at the same time. This discovery forced physics to view the world differently. The new view took the form of quantum mechanics, which is extremely complex.

Quantum mechanics describes particles using wave functions instead of pinning down an exact position and speed. The wave function has a size, which gives a probabilistic idea of where the particle will be found in space. By measuring how the wave function changes between two points, you can estimate the speed of the particle.

In quantum mechanics, particles do not have a sharply defined position and speed.

One problem that quantum mechanics brings is that we can only predict half of the information that classical science provides. We can predict position to a degree of certainty, but not speed, while in classical science we can pin down both. On top of that, quantum theories break down in black holes and other places where the space time continuum is altered.

chapters.title: What can escape a black hole?
chapters.text:
Black holes are regions of space time where gravity is so strong that nothing, no particles, and not even electromagnetic radiation such as light, can escape from them. You may have seen movies where spaceships dodge black holes in interstellar flight. If you strayed from the course of your flight, you might find yourself being pulled in by the gravity of a black hole. Before you became dust, you might be lucky enough to be the first person to see the event horizon of a black hole. You can take some comfort in that.

Black holes form when stars implode. The size of the gravity of the black hole that forms depends on the mass of the collapsing star.

If our sun is a star, why is it not collapsing? You might ask.

A star creates thermal pressure inside itself to balance its own gravity. The nuclear processes inside each star produce so much energy that they keep the star from imploding. When a star runs out of enough nuclear energy from converting hydrogen into helium, it pulls all surrounding matter inward and condenses into a singularity. That singularity is the black hole.

A simple way to picture the event horizon is to imagine sailing a ship close to the edge of Niagara Falls. With enough power, you can keep the ship from going over the precipice. But if you cannot navigate around it, that is the end of life for you and your crew.

If the universe has been around for billions of years and nothing can escape a black hole, the universe should be inside black holes by now. Should it not be? A plausible answer is that some things can escape a black hole.

Stephen Hawking showed in 1974 that black holes release particles at a steady rate. Using quantum mechanics, he argued that particles and antiparticles exist in space. They constantly bond, break apart, and annihilate each other.

When particles and antiparticles go through these complex processes, they interact with black holes. When the event horizon separates particles from antiparticles, it slowly eats away at black holes. The black holes lose mass and shrink. After enough time, the black holes disappear into nothingness.

chapters.title: Technology and political will must combine to fight the prospect of human extinction
chapters.text:
Planet Earth is under a number of threats that make you wonder how it has survived this long. These threats fall into two broad categories: events beyond our control, and events we can control.

The laws of physics and probability have shown us that Earth is overdue for an asteroid collision. Unfortunately, we do not currently have the equipment we would need to protect ourselves from this threat. While we may not be able to prevent an asteroid collision, there are other threats that we can avert. There are problems we can fix.

These fixable issues fall into two broad categories: climate change and nuclear annihilation.

The ecology of our planet tells us that we cannot keep going at these rates of emissions. Ocean temperatures are rising, and that releases more carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. Ice caps are melting and breaking up quickly. The planet is hotter because not enough solar energy is being reflected back into space.

If the carbon footprint is not actively reduced, Earth could end up becoming another Venus, with extreme temperatures. Even Mercury is not as hot as Venus, despite its proximity to the sun.

The second fixable threat is greater than the first. It is nuclear annihilation. The Cold War has ended, but countries still have deep rooted trust issues. More countries keep making nuclear weapons. As it stands, the global nuclear stockpile can destroy the entire planet several times over. We can only hope that humans will have developed the technology to escape the planet and survive the looming disaster.

While either nuclear annihilation or climate change may eventually destroy Earth, it will likely take at least a thousand years for that to happen.

chapters.title: Humans have enough mental capacity to make the universe a better place
chapters.text:
In 1492, when Christopher Columbus decided to sail west, he was ridiculed and the mission was harshly condemned. Several decades later, his discovery of Newfoundland changed the world.

Fast forward five centuries and we find ourselves repeating the same cycle. This time, the target is space. The argument goes that we have too many things to worry about on our own planet. Why should we waste money on space probes? Space agencies are terribly underfunded, and politicians are responsible for that.

But as humans, we are deeply curious creatures, and we keep denying the urge to explore new worlds and travel the universe. To rekindle our longing for exploration, we can take a cue from former US president John F. Kennedy, who set a deadline for a manned lunar landing by the end of the decade. Achieving that feat in 1969 revolutionized the world. Children wanted to become scientists, and many people were inspired and humbled by it.

We can have a base on the moon by 2050 and send humans to Mars by 2070 if we put our minds to it. Reaching those goals will rekindle love for space programs.

While we may not explore outer planets in our lifetimes because of the travel time, we can certainly reach the planets in our own solar system within the next hundred years.

Alpha Centauri is the nearest solar system to ours. It takes 4.5 light years to get there from here. We do not yet have the speed and the means to cross such distances. Perhaps matter antimatter annihilation will provide the energy we need to reach the speeds required to get to Alpha Centauri. For now, we may have to stay in our solar system and make the most of it by exploring the other planets around us.

We need to invest heavily in space exploration so that if a worldwide crisis makes this planet unsuitable, we have an alternative home.

For now, we can use unmanned ships to explore distant solar systems. One example is Project Breakthrough Starshot. The aircraft carries 1000 nanocraft, tiny space probes, into orbit. Gigawatt lasers fired from the Earth based station hit their sails at a specific altitude. Project Starshot is far from finished, but it is doable. We have the genius needed to make it happen.

chapters.title: AI is useful, but it must be carefully managed so it does not replace humans in the workforce
chapters.text:
Hollywood movies have painted a picture of advanced artificial intelligence, AI, created by humans and rebelling against them, trying to take over the planet. Science fiction has shaped this image and also made it seem impossible. But is it really? Even if we agree that Arnold Schwarzenegger is not a machine, we should not laugh off the possibility of super intelligent machines challenging human authority.

Right now, our brains are more advanced than the most advanced computers. At the same time, the ability to adapt based on circumstances is itself a form of intelligence. So an artificial intelligence system can keep improving until it becomes more advanced than humans, leaving our intelligence looking like that of an earthworm compared to what AI will be able to do.

There is a lot of good that can come from AI. With it, we can wipe out diseases, lift people out of poverty, and discover things we never thought we could discover.

Moore Law suggests that computers can double their speed and capacity every 18 months. If that trend continues, computers can keep improving until AI outperforms humans and becomes the superior intelligence.

How do we make sure we are not enslaved by AI? The first step has already been taken. Awareness of this possibility is the starting point. Elon Musk, Stephen Hawking, and a host of AI experts signed an open letter in 2015 to warn of the dangers of uncontrolled superintelligence.

Another good move is the fast growing field of AI Ethics in schools. Most importantly, we must put measures in place that keep machines in the service of humans and check the excesses of AI.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Since the dawn of time, humans have tried to answer existential questions. That search has led to the discovery of universal laws, providing answers to some of the questions. But there are more, complex in nature, that we must answer if we are to keep surviving here on Earth.

There is so much to learn and explore about the world, and we have barely scratched the surface. The remarkable speed with which technology has advanced shows that humans are capable of far greater feats. The universe will keep evolving, and we have to follow suit. Although we cannot find all the answers, we can always try to be more innovative and developed.

Generally, we need to invest more in science and technology, and we should also pay attention to the importance of arts and humanities. These fields are essential to our growth as a species. Our actions and the level of commitment we put into making the world a better place heavily influence everything else. Nature relies on us to do the right things for the environment. The way we treat the environment is the way it will treat us. If we fail to evolve, our planet will die a slow, gradual death.

The point of our existence is visible in our actions and reactions to the things around us. While religion and culture play a major part, sentimental opinion and religious bias can hold us back from progressing. That is not to say we should neglect those aspects, but the best way to move forward is to study our universe, do research, and explore in the safest way possible.

A small step to try. With AI, cars will drive themselves, and we will be launched into a new era of technology. Think about how this change might affect you personally and how it will shift the way we see the world in the future. Also, find ways to keep your environment clean and healthy. If you have read this far, the work has already started.
